title: Credential Format Guide
summary: Understand card format planning, facility codes, card number ranges, and credential collision risk.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool helps evaluate whether a credential format plan is likely to scale cleanly without card number overlap, format confusion, or enrollment problems.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it before ordering badges, migrating systems, merging sites, or standardizing multiple credential populations.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical access-control terms.

items.label: Credential Format
items.description: The bit structure or card technology used by the system.
items.whyItMatters: Format determines what data the panel expects from the card.
items.commonMistake: Mixing formats without documenting which readers and panels support them.

items.label: Facility Code
items.description: A site or organization code embedded in many card formats.
items.whyItMatters: Facility codes help separate card populations but can collide across sites.
items.commonMistake: Reusing a facility code without checking existing cards.

items.label: Card Number Range
items.description: The badge/card numbers assigned to users.
items.whyItMatters: Ranges help avoid duplicate cards and simplify ordering.
items.commonMistake: Ordering new cards without tracking previous ranges.

items.label: Site Count
items.description: How many buildings, branches, or organizations share the credential plan.
items.whyItMatters: More sites make standardization and collision prevention more important.
items.commonMistake: Treating every site independently without a shared credential policy.

items.label: Reader Compatibility
items.description: Whether the physical readers support the credential technology and format.
items.whyItMatters: Unsupported formats may read incorrectly or not at all.
items.commonMistake: Assuming all readers support the same credential type.

items.label: Migration Risk
items.description: The chance that old and new credentials will need to coexist.
items.whyItMatters: Migration periods often create the most confusion.
items.commonMistake: Cutting over without a documented old/new credential strategy.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the credential plan is likely clean. Watch means format/card-range review is needed. Risk means there may be overlap, migration trouble, or long-term enrollment issues.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: simplify the design, add capacity, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most access-control design problems come from underestimating how hardware, users, schedules, exceptions, and maintenance effort interact over time.
bullets: Designing only for the initial install instead of the system lifecycle.
Ignoring exceptions, expansion, or administrative workload.
Assuming manufacturer capacity limits are the same as practical design limits.
Failing to document assumptions before the system is built.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, code review, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer limits still matter.
Local code and life-safety requirements still apply.
Site conditions may change the final design.
A simpler design is usually easier to support, audit, and explain.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, code compliance review, manufacturer validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual product and platform being installed.
Confirm owner requirements and operating procedures.
Document assumptions before implementation.
